speaker: interviewer
text: 1995. When you were living in Prague before the deportation, describe life in the Jewish sector of Prague under the Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to hand in our radios. We were not allowed to leave-- to be on the street-- I guess after 8 o'clock or after darkness. We had to put our Star of David on our clothing. We were not allowed to ride-- to be in a-- in a bus or streetcar. There were restrictions and restrictions. So that's about all I can tell. Deportations had started and always 1,000 people were taken to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how were the selections made for deportation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I bet pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they decide who would be deported?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I don't know that. The people were summoned on a 24-hour basis, and they had to come to Veletrzni Palac. This was a something like we had here where they display merchandise, big halls. And in one of these halls, there were-- there was a-- by-- by chalk or by paint, a mark 3 by 6 feet-- a square, so to speak-- and there was a number on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was my number, 558, and as soon as I stepped in-- into the place, they gave me-- [BACKROUND NOISE] they gave me a-- a-- a string on my neck, and on it was a badge, and it says, AM. That's the name of the transport-- every transport had a different name or a letter, starting by A, and then AA, AB, and AM. I was AM. And 558 was on this badge.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I walked in, and there I see-- on that 3 by 6-- I see this is my place, and it says 558. So I put my little suitcase there, and this is where I'm going to sleep overnight. And in the morning, they put us in the train and took us 50 kilometers away from Prague was Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I'm just going to go back again for a moment. What was the Jewish government like in Prague at this time? What kind of institutions were in place to-- within the Jewish community? Was a Counsel of Elders? Who-- who ran--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there was a Council of Elders, but there-- they had offices there, but I think they were just powerless. They were in-- they had to do whatever the Germans told them to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the situation in terms of getting food? Was food available?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everything was rationed in Prague in those days. There wasn't free that you could go in and buy. Everything was rationed. The milk was rationed. Butter, I guess, I forget. Maybe there was not even any butter. Bread was rationed. And victuals, I-- I guess you had to-- unless you had a friend who was in the store and then they-- if they liked you or were friendly to you they'd put something under the-- into the shopping bag. So they stole it away and gave it to you. You had to pay for it, but of course. They broke the law that, you know, they should be rationed. So it was-- it was restrictions food-wise and many other ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do any work at all during that time? You were teaching.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was teaching--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --until this was disbanded.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then you-- you were summoned for deportation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] April 28, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And would you describe to us-- when you were summoned, how many people were summoned with you?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you were taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to Theresienstadt. And we were embarked from the train, and Theresienstadt had 4,000 inhabitants. It was a garrison-- military garrison. It was on the border of Germany and Czechoslovakia. Maria Theresa, she was a queen in 1740 to 1780, who was the queen of Austro-Hungarian monarchy. And she built this city called Theresienstadt after her-- named after Maria Theresa, Theresa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what you recall when you got off the train. What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were some helpers. There were some helpers who'd take our suitcase. And they took us-- the train stopped and-- and shortly after, my-- my roommates, who was the architect-- Max Spiegel-- he had a girlfriend, Hilda. She was in the same transport-- AM transport-- with me. And Max told her, I have a friend in Theresienstadt who is in the Jewish government. His name is Dr.-- he was a lawyer; in the old country, lawyers are called doctors-- Dr. Pauper. Egan Pauper. Egan Pauper. He said, when you get to Theresienstadt, find him, and he will help you there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lo and behold, in the train station, Hilda was walking with the man who didn't have a badge, and she called me over, Albert, come over here. This is Dr. Pauper. Remember Max told us about him? And this Dr. Pauper said, well, I'm in the Jewish government here, and I'll take your number. He wrote down my number.

speaker: interviewee
text: And out of 1,000 people, about 500 were next day, next morning taking another train, and taken to the east. If you didn't know what east means, that was Poland. We didn't know what it means. They told us labor camp. Just labor. And I was still in the old place-- in the old station-- and a man came to me and said, I am a policeman. I was sent from Dr. Pauper to take you to the government office. So I got out with this policeman, and I was taken to a barracks with 30 people. I-- I think this-- I was already in the barracks, and this man came to talk to me. He said, I have to take you to Dr. Pauper. I came to Dr. Pauper-- because the gendarmes and the Germans were at the-- at the gates, so we couldn't get out. But this man had a-- a piece of paper from the Jewish government for Dr. Pauper to let us out. He took me to Egan-- the Dr. Pauper. And Pauper said, well, I want to-- want you to stay here to keep you here. I want you to be a policeman-- there were 300 ghetto policemen-- but the condition is that you would have to be in the army. And so, lo and behold, I-- I was selected to be in the army, but I postponement on my-- my university studies. Oh, never mind, he said. Go and see Captain [? Klaber. ?] He's about three doors from me. Tell him I sent you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I knocked on the door. Captain [? Klaber, ?] I said, I'm sent for Dr.-- from Dr.-- Dr. Egan Pauper. Yeah, and he said, I'm selecting men for police. Were you in the army? I said, I wasn't. No, I said I was. So I had to lie. He said, where-- where-- where did you serve? I said, the 26th infantry in Banska Bystrica. He looked at me. Really, he said. Is Captain So-and-So still there? I said, oh gee. He got my lie. He saw my face. He said, that's all right. I understand. So, stand straight, salute. I saluted. Make a right turn. I made a right. Make a left turn. OK, you're in. So I passed. I was a policeman. And it was a big thing because you were protected from the east, on the transport to the east. Your parents were protected. Your wife was protected. Your girl-- your fiance was protected, and I had Vera in Prague. She was mixed marriage. But the later on-- from-- she was half Jewish. Later on, they took all-- all of these mixed marriages-- mixed kids, too-- to Theresienstadt. I said, in case she comes, I put her on my list. And lo and behold, she came to Theresienstadt. And I went to the office and who was sitting there? Dr. Fundle. He was one of the lawyers who was out of the three lawyers teaching with me. I said, Otto, Vera came. Can I put her with me as my fiancee on the-- on the police plate so she would be protected? OK, I'll do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the uprising was in Warsaw ghetto, and the-- the Germans said that the Jewish policemen in the Warsaw ghetto were smuggling with the police-- with the Polish police-- smuggling arms into the ghetto. And they disbanded the police in Warsaw ghetto-- the Jewish police-- and every ghetto all over. So we were disbanded.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? What date? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year was '44, I guess. '44. And all my friends in the police-- all my comrades, you know, buddies-- had a summons, and I didn't get one. So I ran up to Otto Fundle, you know. I said, Otto, what's going on here? I want to go with my friends. He said, no. I can't send you. He said, no, because your-- your [GERMAN]. Your fiancee is half Jewish and you have to stay with her, and I can't send any half Jews into the transport. I was protected. She saved my life. Because all these, after the war, only six of us came back. Six of the policemen. They were all transported and gassed. Whew.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were a policeman, what were your duties as a policeman?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a policeman? Well, we had a sort of uniform. You know what the French policemen look like with the hat? Like a square hat? And that's what we wore. [BACKROUND NOISE] We had a-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] Okay. We had a-- a belt across our-- our midriff, and a-- a-- a piece of a-- a leather strip across the shoulder. And we had a wooden stick about the size of about a quarter of a meter. And with the handle-- with the-- a-- a rubber handle, a rubber-- rubber- a piece of rubber holding to our hands. And that was our only weapon we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your duties?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, duty-- the duties were-- there were six or-- six at least six of these barracks where the army was originally, army was housed. A lot of these barracks had maybe holding eight, seven hundred soldiers. So what we did was evacuate-- taken away-- and this was made a ubication for the Jews who came from Slovakia, Czechoslovakia, from Germany, from Holland, from-- from all-- all kind of small countries, over Europe, really was-- except Poland. Hungary-- Hungarian Jews came, Slovakian Jew, I told you that, Czechoslovakian Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did the policemen do? What did the Jewish policemen do?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, we were-- we were stationed in these barracks at the gate, and everybody had to have a reason to walk through, and we checked these people, whether they have the right to be-- if they live there-- so we let them in. And there were kitchens where these people-- the Jews-- there were maybe in one barrack 800 people, or thousands of people, maybe, housed. Because at one time, Theresienstadt had about 40,000, maybe even 60,000, Jews there. And gradually, almost every second, there were transfers going, trains taking them to the east. Although we didn't know what east meant. OK. So the kitchens were there, and they were feeding people lined up here with a piece of dish, and the cooks were putting the food in, but not much to-- to food. But the-- the cooks were stealing the food, taking it home to their families or themselves, and we checked them at the door. And they bribed us with the food, so we were well-fed. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Theresienstadt was a Muster ghetto-- it was a-- a sample ghetto. We had opera. We had concerts. Not much food. People were dying. Old people were dying for not having enough nutrition, and there was typhus and there was sicknesses, mainly old people. There was a farming community around. Jews were working on the farm around and the Czech gendarmes were guarding us and the German SS were in the background. And we had-- at one time, we had in the police a-- a German Jew who was commanding us. He was from Berlin. And 300 of us were try-- were doing the goose step, you know, because he was sort of a German policeman back home and he taught us that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- we had concerts. We had-- we had-- they invited the Red Cross from Switzerland to-- to see us, how well we lived, so there were sometimes sardines on the street you could buy while the Red Cross was there. When was that? When did the Red Cross come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh maybe end of '43? Because in '44, I was put into a transport, which I wanted to avoid because there was encephalitis in the camp. Young Jewish girls had this encephalitis. You heard of this? They call it maybe enkephalitis.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a brain-- brain thick-- sort of a sleeping sickness. These people couldn't walk straight. They were falling these young kids to the one side. And-- and a friend of mine had it and she told me the symptoms. As I was summonsed to this transport-- I didn't want to go there-- [BACKROUND NOISE] so I said, I'm going to-- I'm going to pretend that I have this sickness-- the encephalitis. So they-- they took me-- several doctors examined me, and I pretended-- I knew the symptoms, how to fall over-- and they found out that I am pretending, that I am faking this. So they put me into the transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: So 200 of us went to East Germany to build a barracks there for a high-- German high command there in a forest. It was in Wulkow in East Germany. And as we embarked, 200 of us, wooden train with parts of wooden barracks to build a barracks there. We embarked there in a [INAUDIBLE]. It was wintertime. There was snow on the ground. We were in a [INAUDIBLE] standing in a semicircle, and an officer was talking to us. He said, I'm Adolf Eichmann. You're going to build-- stay with us and build for us here. [INAUDIBLE]. All right? We win the war, you stay for us all your life. Whew. This is Adolf Eichmann. You know-- Did you know who Adolf Eichmann was at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- no, oh no.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. I didn't. I didn't really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Up to this is time--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: --had you had any news from home? Did you know what was happening outside of Theresienstadt or what had happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not while I was in Prague, my mother wrote to me, don't come home because all your friends are taken into the army. She wanted me to avoid the Hungarian army. You know, mother [INAUDIBLE]. She wanted me not to be in the army because it's dangerous, but that was it. That was the last time I heard from them. The [INAUDIBLE], and they were deported and that was the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in Theresienstadt, what news did you have of what was happening outside? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No news at all. No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, when I arrived in Theresienstadt, there was-- there was a-- there was talking about that some young Jewish men had connections with the outside world, were sending letters out to the Aryan Czech population, were even getting some mail from the outside. And they were caught, and 12 of them were hanged publicly. And I knew the man who was-- he was a butcher, and he was assigned to do the hanging for them. Fischer was his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was German?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he did the--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a member of the-- but he was mentally disturbed. And they asked him to-- to do the executions. And there were publicly 12 of them executed, but I wasn't there. I came after this happened-- shortly after. But everybody told us about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other incidents while you were in Theresienstadt? Anything that stands--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --out in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was an incident. One day they had the whole ghetto moved about three kilometers from the ghetto, and we were in a valley standing, this-- it could have been a big pond before, but there was no water in it-- and it was a-- a little-- not-- like hills around the whole thing. Well, it dried out. The water was taken out or dried out. We were standing there, the whole of Theresienstadt, I don't know how many-- 1,000 of us-- standing all day. And there were-- there were soldiers on the embankment with guns facing us. And allegedly, we found out, towards the evening, that the Germans were talking to the-- this is what I heard-- to the Red Cross in Switzerland to buy our live out for money. And they were arguing back and forth. By the end-- by the evening, I guess the deal came over and we were asked to march back into the ghetto. Everybody except the sick who were not able to walk, who stayed in the ghetto, and us coming out and going back and we were safe then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you got back, you just went back to your routine? What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess this-- this happened before I went to build the barracks. In the barracks, there were 200 of us, and the-- the chief, the MP, the military-- the-- the CO-- commanding officer-- was Sturmfuhrer Stuchka and he was a sadist in a way.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at one time, six of us were building-- digging ditches, and it was November. It was raining, and I put my jacket on. And another fellow put his jacket on. The others had their jacket-- not on-- and unexpectedly, Stuchka, the CO, came by and saw me having the jacket on. And he said, take the jacket off. I took the jacket off. That was it. I thought the-- the reprimand was big enough. Came 6 o'clock, we are lined up for-- for-- to be accounted, you know, for, all 200 of us. What is it called? Zahlappell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the Zahlappell? The line up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Line up, where they count when everybody is there. And after, he said, dismissed except those two who had those jackets on. So we two stayed back, standing there alone with him. And he called the German with the gun-- the, whatever, the Gestapo man. SS man he called over, take them to the gate, these two. That was us. We had to take everything off except leaving our pants, so we were without the shirt on. It was raining. It was November. Whatever, it was cold. And we didn't know what's going to happen. The guy with the gun standing with us, too. Freezing, oh boy. We rubbed each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, come about 11 o'clock, Stuchka came by. I said, did you have enough? I said, yes. [GERMAN]. That means get off. And the-- the guy-- the guy with the gun, the SS man, followed us to the ubication where we were sleeping. And we were on bunk beds. Never in my life I've had a warmer time than to huddle with my buddy next to me. It was a 19-year-old boy from Vienna, and he kept me warm. He embraced me there, and I kept warm. Boy, that was good. Those are the two things I meant to tell you from Theresienstadt. Oh, but then in-- in that-- in this camp, in Wulkow, we had-- we had wooden shoes, wooden shoes with a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sorry? Yeah-- and I infected my foot. And there were no medication there, so it started to-- it started to be infected. And I was taken to the sick bay. There were six of us there. And there was a doctor. The one and only doctor we had. He was dentist-- he was a-- he was a dentist. Dr. Minz was his name. He was with us six there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we heard-- we heard gunfire, gun shooting, coming louder and [INAUDIBLE] all the while I was in the sick bay. And there was no medication to do anything-- just to rest up. And we knew that the Russians are coming. This was '44, February-- '45. Pardon me, '45-- February '45. The Russians were coming and one of night, Stuchka came in and asked the doctor, Dr. Minz, said, tell me-- point out those who can walk. Well, I couldn't walk. But Minz-- Dr. Minz was on friendly terms with me. He pointed me out and two others. He said, leave the place immediately. And Dr. Minz went-- went with us. And I don't know what happened to the-- to the rest. Never seen them after, the other three. And from there, we marched. This friend-- this 19-year-old Viennese boy-- was holding me, and I went one foot for six kilometers into the next station. They put us into a train, and in that train, we were 11 days going back to Theresienstadt. And just one place they stopped and gave us some food. We were in one-- in-- in-- in a cattle train.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in the car with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were several-- about three or four-- these-- of these cattle cars. I was in one. And there was no food. There was just a pail for, you know, for the necessities. And then-- there was just a little opening with a-- with steel rods in like a window. And they took us through Germany, and we saw that the German army-- people were coming back wounded with trains. We stopped in many stations. It took us 11-- 11 days to come back to Theresienstadt. And we were so-- when we got out of the train we couldn't recognize each other because we lost so much weight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you any food at all? What did you eat? What did you survive on?

speaker: interviewee
text: They fed us once in one station. They gave us some food-- soup or something. We had to line up. But you could hardly eat because your stomach was empty.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in the train with you? Or on the-- in your car?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 40, 45. About that. So about three or four holding the 200.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were people who were coming back from--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Wulkow.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from Wulkow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There's two guys escaped. And they were then shortly after-- you know, from this Wulkow camp-- they got out. They were German Jews. They were from Berlin and they spoke the language. But they caught them. And at one roll call, it was announced that two were caught. And they were kept somewhere in the bunker. I didn't see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? Before you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before we left, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in Wulkow altogether?

speaker: interviewer
text: The 200?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how much work did you actually get done?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we built 32 barracks-- wooden barracks-- prefab barracks-- in a forest. We cut out the trees and put in pylons into the-- which was-- Sorry? You want me to stop?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're gonna pause for just a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
